fact_summary: A computer transforms data into meaningful information. Its five basic operations are input, storage, processing, output, and control. Input enters data and programs; memory stores data and instructions; output produces useful results. The control unit supervises input, output, processing, and storage. The arithmetic logic unit performs arithmetic, logic, and comparison operations. The ALU and control unit together form the central processing unit. Major computer characteristics include speed, accuracy, diligence, versatility, and storage capacity.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Manually cross-checked against sections 1.3 and 1.3.1 of the NIOS lesson; only stable foundational statements were retained.

fact_summary: Computer components are divided into hardware and software. Hardware is the physical machine and its connected devices. Peripheral devices connect externally for specific functions. Keyboard, mouse, scanner, touch screen, and microphone are input devices; monitor, printer, plotter, and speakers are output devices. A program is an ordered set of instructions, while software is a set of programs that makes hardware perform tasks. System software runs the computer, controls operations, and manages resources. Operating systems and utilities are system software. Application software performs specific user tasks and may be general-purpose or customized.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Manually cross-checked against sections 1.4 through 1.7.2 of the NIOS lesson; obsolete device performance figures were excluded.

fact_summary: An operating system is system software. It provides an interface through which the user communicates with the computer. It manages hardware devices such as disks, keyboards, and monitors; manages and maintains disk file systems; supports application programs; controls computer operations; and helps manage computer resources. Utility programs supplement operating-system functionality; examples include file compression and decompression tools, antivirus tools, and file split or join tools.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Manually cross-checked against section 1.7.1 of the NIOS lesson.

fact_summary: A graphical user interface displays commands visually, including as icons. A taskbar can represent running programs or open windows and allow switching between them. File management organizes files, folders, and storage drives hierarchically. File-management tools can copy, move, rename, search for, create, and delete files or folders. A recycle bin temporarily retains certain deleted files until it is emptied and can allow restoration. System settings tools configure the computer and manage software or hardware installation. Search tools can locate files and folders by name, location, date, size, or other criteria.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Manually cross-checked against sections 2.3 through 2.6; version-specific Windows XP sequences were excluded from the fact set.

fact_summary: Data communication is the exchange of digital data between computers. A computer network is a group of interconnected computers that exchange and share information; connections may be wired or wireless. A communication system includes a transmitter, channel, and receiver. Simplex sends in one direction, half duplex sends in both directions but not simultaneously, and full duplex sends and receives simultaneously. Bandwidth is the range of frequencies that make up a signal. A modem converts digital data to analog and analog data to digital. LAN, MAN, and WAN describe networks of increasing geographic scope, and the Internet is a public WAN. Network protocols define communication rules and conventions. HTTP uses a client-server model, while HTTPS supports secure communication over a computer network.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Manually cross-checked against sections 5.1 through 5.6 of the NIOS lesson.

fact_summary: Microsoft Office basics apply across Word, Excel, and PowerPoint. Templates can be used to create professional documents. SmartArt and charts can express complex ideas and data. Shared documents support collaboration while participants work offline, online, or simultaneously. Office apps are available across PC, Mac, and mobile devices, and users can continue work across supported devices. Built-in help and training can assist users in finding commands and learning tasks.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Cross-checked against the overview and applicability list on Microsoft Support.

fact_summary: Microsoft Word supports creating and working with documents. Its core task areas include formatting text, controlling pages and layouts, inserting pictures, saving and printing, and performing mail merge. Word also supports reviewing through Track Changes and sharing documents for collaboration. Document formatting includes font, capitalization, page numbering, and page orientation controls. Charts and WordArt can be added to a document.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Cross-checked against the Word Help and Learning task categories and featured guidance.

fact_summary: Microsoft Excel works with workbooks and spreadsheets. A blank workbook can be created from Excel or with Ctrl+N. AutoFill can streamline data entry. Excel can recommend charts based on selected data and create a chart from that data. Data bars, color coding, and icons can reveal trends and patterns. Excel supports entering and formatting data, calculating values, organizing selected data as tables, filtering and sorting, computing totals such as sum, average, or count, and visualizing data in charts.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Cross-checked against Microsoft's What is Excel and linked basic-task guidance.

source_title: Quick tips: Create and open a presentation in PowerPoint for the web
fact_summary: Microsoft PowerPoint is used to create and work with presentations. A presentation can start as a blank file, a recent file, or from a theme. A presentation is composed of slides, and a new slide can be inserted after a selected slide with a chosen layout. A presentation can be named or renamed. PowerPoint for the web automatically saves work in OneDrive, supports sharing and collaboration, can create an online duplicate, and can download a copy to the user's device. Pending online changes synchronize after connectivity returns.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Cross-checked against Microsoft's create, add-slide, save, and offline sections.

fact_summary: A database is an organized collection of data, and a DBMS is software used to manage computerized databases. A relational database organizes data in two-dimensional tables called relations. A table contains rows, also called tuples or records, and columns, also called attributes or fields. A schema describes the logical organization of a database. An entity is a person or physical or logical object about which data is stored. Cardinality is the number of rows in a relation, while degree is the number of columns. A domain is the pool of permitted values for a column. A primary key uniquely identifies each row; candidate keys are possible primary keys; a foreign key establishes a relationship with another table. Normalization structures data into separate relational tables to improve retrieval and maintenance.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Manually cross-checked against sections 23.1 and the normalization introduction of the NIOS lesson.

fact_summary: Cybersecurity uses policies and procedures to protect information and information systems. A cyberattack is an intrusion whose immediate concern is damage or disruption. Cyber espionage is intrusion intended to obtain information. Cyber sabotage targets computers or connected systems to disrupt equipment or services. Cyber propaganda uses digital channels such as social media and fake websites to shape perceptions and influence public opinion. Cybercrime involves the use or knowledge of computers to cause harm. Networked systems and critical services can be vulnerable to cyber disruption, so defensive planning aims to reduce vulnerability, limit damage, and shorten recovery time.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Manually cross-checked against sections 15.1 through 15.3; historical examples were excluded.

source_title: Cyber Security Awareness Booklet for Citizens
fact_summary: Use a strong, unique password for each account and device; combine letters, numbers, and special characters, and do not share passwords or PINs. Use two-step verification whenever possible. Do not save usernames and passwords in a browser on shared systems, and log out after use. Do not click suspicious email or SMS links or open untrusted attachments. Install software only from known trusted sources, keep systems patched, and keep antivirus and firewall protection enabled and current. Scan external devices before use. Avoid sensitive logins on public Wi-Fi. Never disclose banking passwords, OTPs, PINs, card numbers, or CVVs to callers. Report suspected compromise promptly through appropriate authorities.
verification_notes: Pilot draft. Manually cross-checked against the password, phishing, virus-protection, general-safety, and incident-reporting sections of the Ministry of Home Affairs portal booklet.
